SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks and compares to relevant baselines, reports F1 and predicate-level accuracy, and achieves substantial gains. It analyzes contributions of constrained decoding and training techniques (highway connections, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization), indicating ablation-style attribution of improvements, and reports ensemble results. Related work is described as adequate, failure modes and limitations (adjunct/argument confusion, PP attachment, constraint violations) are explicitly discussed, and code plus hyperparameters are provided for reproducibility—supporting a comprehensive and thorough experimental evaluation.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (deep highway BiLSTM, constrained A* decoding, ensemble gains) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions are consistent and hyperparameters/code are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples supporting results on CoNLL-2005/2012 are informative, and the writing is generally free of ambiguity and grammatical issues, though related work coverage is described as merely adequate rather than exemplary, suggesting only minor presentation shortcomings.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear well supported by experiments on standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks with appropriate baselines, reported hyperparameters, and released code (supporting reproducibility). The proposed model design choices (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization) and constrained A* decoding are analyzed and tied to empirical gains, and the ensemble results show the reported F1 improvements. Minor technical concerns remain—no mention of statistical significance testing and some SRL constraints were deliberately not enforced—so while the experimental methodology is strong, these omissions prevent a perfect technical score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is a strong fit: semantic role labeling is a core NLP task and the paper presents clearly relevant ML advances (deep highway BiLSTMs, constrained A* decoding, product-of-experts ensemble) with state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012, 83.2/83.4 F1). The work uses expected methods and evaluation (standard baselines, F1, error analysis, code released, hyperparameters reported) and discusses practical limitations and connections to syntactic information that are of clear interest to the venue's community. Overall the topic, methods, datasets, and positioning align well with a top-tier NLP/ML conference audience.

IMPACT.justification: The paper targets an important problem (span-based SRL) and delivers substantial empirical gains — an 8-layer ensemble reaches 83.2 F1 (CoNLL-2005) and 83.4 F1 (CoNLL-2012), representing roughly a 10% relative error reduction over prior work. By showing that deep highway BiLSTMs with constrained A* decoding and specific training advances materially improve recovery of long-distance predicate-argument dependencies, it will change how researchers build SRL systems and is likely to be widely adopted and extended. The explicit analysis of remaining error modes (adjunct/argument confusion, PP attachment, constraint violations) and the observation that syntax can still help open clear follow-up research directions, so the impact is strong though not fully transformative across NLP.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to a comprehensive set of established and recent SRL baselines (e.g., Zhou & Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015; and classic syntax-aware systems like Toutanova et al. 2008, Punyakanok et al. 2008), using standard datasets (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012) and evaluation metrics (F1, % completely correct predicates). Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (code and hyperparameters provided), the paper acknowledges when syntax-based methods still help, and it analyzes which components (deep BiLSTM, constrained A* decoding, training tricks) drive gains and remaining error modes. Overall the prior work coverage and analysis are comprehensive and appropriate.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines known techniques (stacked highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization, product-of-experts ensembling) with an admissible A* constrained decoding applied to span-based SRL and demonstrates substantial empirical gains on CoNLL-2005/2012. These choices are meaningful, non-trivial engineering and analysis contributions (notably improved long-distance dependency recovery), but they largely adapt and integrate existing methods rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm. The paper does clearly differentiate which components drive gains and would inform experts, yet its novelty is moderate rather than highly original.

main_claims: A deep highway BiLSTM model with constrained A* decoding substantially improves state-of-the-art span-based SRL performance.
An 8-layer ensemble (product-of-experts over 5 models) achieves 83.2 F1 on CoNLL-2005 and 83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2012, representing roughly a 10% relative error reduction over prior work.
Deep models excel at recovering long-distance predicate-argument dependencies compared to shallow or syntax-based models.
Constrained decoding (BIO and selected SRL constraints) and training advances (highway connections, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization) are key contributors to the gains.

Despite strong end-to-end performance, there remain clear error modes (e.g., adjunct/argument confusion, PP attachment, SRL constraint violations) and explicit syntactic information can still yield further improvements.
_summary.methodology: Train deep (stacked) highway bidirectional LSTMs with recurrent dropout and orthonormal initialization to produce local tag scores, then enforce global structural constraints at prediction time using an admissible A* constrained decoding; combine multiple models via a product-of-experts ensemble.
limitations: Model still makes many errors on adjunct vs. argument distinction.
Failures tied to prepositional phrase (PP) attachment and other common linguistic challenges.
Model predictions can violate SRL-specific global constraints (some constraints like R are commonly violated in gold and were not enforced).
Performance degrades for arguments farther from the predicate (though less so than traditional models).
Explicit syntactic information can still improve results, so end-to-end models have not fully removed the value of parsers.
